scenario: Sable Insurer models a Camunda 8.8 claims handling workflow that handles 7500 claims per day. A boundary event is attached to a subprocess that contains several active inner activities, and the team wants to confirm what happens to those inner activities when the boundary event fires.
question: According to the BPMN primer, what happens to the inner activities of a subprocess when an event attached to its border is triggered?

options.text: The subprocess is interrupted regardless of which inner element is currently active inside the subprocess when the border event is triggered for that scope
The currently active inner element keeps running and the subprocess waits for it to complete before reacting to the border event triggered on its scope
The border event is queued and only delivered after every active inner element of the subprocess completes its own work and the subprocess finishes
The currently active inner element is moved to the outgoing path of the subprocess and continues running there after the border event has been triggered

a.text: Correct. The primer states that when an event attached to a subprocess border is triggered, the subprocess is interrupted regardless of which inner element is currently active at that moment.

b.text: Incorrect. The triggered border event interrupts the subprocess immediately; it does not wait for the active inner element to finish before reacting.

c.text: Incorrect. The border event is not queued until the subprocess finishes; it interrupts the subprocess and the active inner element on trigger.

d.text: Incorrect. Inner elements are not relocated onto the outgoing path on interruption; the subprocess is interrupted and the outgoing path of the boundary event is taken.

explanation: Subprocesses in the BPMN primer act as element containers and accept events on their border. When such an event triggers, the entire subprocess is interrupted regardless of which inner element is currently active.
